exercises.title: Basic Variable Assignment
exercises.description: Create a variable named `name` and assign it your name as a string.
hints: Use the syntax: variable_name = value
String values must be enclosed in quotes (single or double)
exercises.solution: name = "Alice"
exercises.test_code: assert name == 'Alice', f"name should be 'Alice', got {name!r}"

exercises.title: Numeric Variable Assignment
exercises.description: Create a variable named `age` and assign it the value 25.
hints: Numeric values (integers) don't need quotes
The syntax is the same: variable_name = value
exercises.solution: age = 25
exercises.test_code: assert age == 25, f"age should be 25, got {age!r}"

exercises.title: Float Variable Assignment
exercises.description: Create a variable named `height` and assign it the value 5.9 (representing height in feet).
hints: Floating-point numbers have a decimal point
height = 5.9 will automatically create a float
exercises.solution: height = 5.9
exercises.test_code: assert height == 5.9, f"height should be 5.9, got {height!r}"

exercises.title: Boolean Variable Assignment
exercises.description: Create a variable named `is_student` and assign it the boolean value True.
hints: Boolean values are True or False (capital T and F)
These represent true/false conditions
exercises.solution: is_student = True
exercises.test_code: assert is_student == True, f"is_student should be True, got {is_student!r}"

exercises.title: Multiple Variable Assignment
exercises.description: Assign the values 10, 20, and 30 to three variables: `x`, `y`, and `z` using a single line of code.
hints: You can assign multiple variables at once: a, b, c = 1, 2, 3
The order matters: first value to first variable, etc.
exercises.starter_code: # Assign 10, 20, 30 to x, y, z in one line
exercises.test_code:
assert x == 10, f"x should be 10, got {x!r}"
assert y == 20, f"y should be 20, got {y!r}"
assert z == 30, f"z should be 30, got {z!r}"

exercises.title: Variable Swapping
exercises.description: Swap the values of two variables `a` and `b` (a=5, b=10) so that a=10 and b=5.
hints: Python allows you to swap without a temporary variable
Use: a, b = b, a
exercises.solution:
a, b = 5, 10
a, b = b, a
exercises.test_code:
assert a == 10, f"a should be 10, got {a!r}"
assert b == 5, f"b should be 5, got {b!r}"

exercises.title: Variable Naming Conventions
exercises.description: Create variables following snake_case naming convention for a person's first name, last name, and email address.
hints: snake_case uses lowercase letters and underscores (no spaces or hyphens)
Examples: first_name, last_name, email_address
exercises.solution:
first_name = "John"
last_name = "Doe"
email_address = "john.doe@example.com"
exercises.test_code:
assert first_name == 'John', f"first_name should be 'John', got {first_name!r}"
assert last_name == 'Doe', f"last_name should be 'Doe', got {last_name!r}"
assert email_address == 'john.doe@example.com', f"email_address should be 'john.doe@example.com', got {email_address!r}"

exercises.title: Reassigning Variables
exercises.description: Create a variable `count` with value 0, then reassign it to 10.
hints: First assignment: count = 0
Then reassign: count = 10
exercises.solution:
count = 0
count = 10
exercises.test_code: assert count == 10, f"count should be 10, got {count!r}"

exercises.title: None Type Variable
exercises.description: Create a variable named `result` and assign it None (representing no value).
hints: None is a special value (not a string, not 0, not empty)
result = None
exercises.solution: result = None
exercises.starter_code: # Create a variable with None value
exercises.test_code: assert result == None, f"result should be None, got {result!r}"

exercises.title: Constant Naming Convention
exercises.description: Create a constant variable for the speed of light (299792458 m/s) using ALL_CAPS naming convention.
hints: Constants are named in ALL_CAPS with underscores
Example: SPEED_OF_LIGHT = 299792458
exercises.solution: SPEED_OF_LIGHT = 299792458
exercises.starter_code: # Create a constant for speed of light
exercises.test_code: assert SPEED_OF_LIGHT == 299792458, f"SPEED_OF_LIGHT should be 299792458, got {SPEED_OF_LIGHT!r}"

exercises.title: String Concatenation
exercises.description: Create two string variables `greeting` and `name`, then concatenate them with a space to create a full greeting.
hints: Use the + operator to concatenate strings
greeting + ' ' + name
exercises.solution:
greeting = "Hello"
name = "Alice"
full_greeting = greeting + ' ' + name
exercises.test_code:
assert greeting == 'Hello', f"greeting should be 'Hello', got {greeting!r}"
assert name == 'Alice', f"name should be 'Alice', got {name!r}"
assert full_greeting == 'Hello Alice', f"full_greeting should be 'Hello Alice', got {full_greeting!r}"

exercises.title: Augmented Assignment (+=)
exercises.description: Start with `total = 100`, then add 50 to it using the += operator.
hints: total += 50 is shorthand for total = total + 50
Augmented assignment operators work with other operators too (*=, -=, etc.)
exercises.solution:
total = 100
total += 50
exercises.test_code: assert total == 150, f"total should be 150, got {total!r}"

exercises.title: F-String Formatting
exercises.description: Create variables `name` and `age`, then use an f-string to print 'Name: [name], Age: [age]'.
hints: F-strings use f" " syntax
Inside f-strings, use {variable_name} to insert values
exercises.solution:
name = "Bob"
age = 30
message = f"Name: {name}, Age: {age}"
exercises.starter_code:
name = "Bob"
age = 30
# Use f-string to create message
exercises.test_code:
assert name == 'Bob', f"name should be 'Bob', got {name!r}"
assert age == 30, f"age should be 30, got {age!r}"
assert message == 'Name: Bob, Age: 30', f"message should be 'Name: Bob, Age: 30', got {message!r}"

exercises.title: Type Inference in Python
exercises.description: Create three variables with different types (string, integer, float), then verify each type using the type() function.
hints: Python automatically infers the type based on the value
You can check types later: type(variable) returns <class 'type_name'>
exercises.solution:
text = "hello"
num_int = 42
num_float = 3.14
type_text = type(text)
type_int = type(num_int)
type_float = type(num_float)
exercises.test_code:
assert text == 'hello', f"text should be 'hello', got {text!r}"
assert num_int == 42, f"num_int should be 42, got {num_int!r}"
assert num_float == 3.14, f"num_float should be 3.14, got {num_float!r}"
assert 'type_text' in dir(), "'type_text' is not defined"
assert callable(type_text), "'type_text' should be a function"
assert 'type_int' in dir(), "'type_int' is not defined"
assert callable(type_int), "'type_int' should be a function"
assert 'type_float' in dir(), "'type_float' is not defined"
assert callable(type_float), "'type_float' should be a function"

exercises.title: Variable Reassignment with Calculation
exercises.description: Create a variable `price = 100`, then reassign it by adding 25% tax to it.
hints: price = price + (price * 0.25) or price *= 1.25
exercises.solution:
price = 100
price = price * 1.25
exercises.test_code: assert price == 125.0, f"price should be 125.0, got {price!r}"

exercises.title: Multi-line String Variables
exercises.description: Create a variable `poem` containing a multi-line string (at least 3 lines).
hints: Use triple quotes for multi-line strings: """ ... """
Or use \n escape sequence for newlines
exercises.solution: poem = """Line one\nLine two\nLine three"""
exercises.test_code:
assert poem == 'Line one
Line two
Line three', f"poem should be 'Line one
Line two
Line three', got {poem!r}"

exercises.title: Using Underscore as Throwaway Variable
exercises.description: Unpack a tuple (5, 10) into `x` and `_` (throwaway variable), ignoring the second value.
hints: Use _ when you don't care about a value
exercises.test_code: assert x == 5, f"x should be 5, got {x!r}"

exercises.title: Variable in Expression
exercises.description: Create variables `base = 5` and `exponent = 3`, then create `result` = base^exponent (5 to the power of 3).
hints: Use the ** operator for exponentiation
result = base ** exponent
exercises.solution:
base = 5
exponent = 3
result = base ** exponent
exercises.test_code:
assert base == 5, f"base should be 5, got {base!r}"
assert exponent == 3, f"exponent should be 3, got {exponent!r}"
assert result == 125, f"result should be 125, got {result!r}"

exercises.title: Real-World: Restaurant Bill
exercises.description: Create variables for a restaurant bill: `subtotal = 50`, `tax_rate = 0.1`, `tip_pct = 0.15`, then calculate the final total including tax and tip.
hints: Total = subtotal + tax + tip
tax = subtotal * tax_rate; tip = subtotal * tip_pct
exercises.solution:
subtotal = 50
tax_rate = 0.1
tip_pct = 0.15
tax = subtotal * tax_rate
tip = subtotal * tip_pct
total = subtotal + tax + tip
exercises.test_code:
assert subtotal == 50, f"subtotal should be 50, got {subtotal!r}"
assert tax_rate == 0.1, f"tax_rate should be 0.1, got {tax_rate!r}"
assert tip_pct == 0.15, f"tip_pct should be 0.15, got {tip_pct!r}"
assert tax == 5.0, f"tax should be 5.0, got {tax!r}"
assert tip == 7.5, f"tip should be 7.5, got {tip!r}"
assert total == 62.5, f"total should be 62.5, got {total!r}"

exercises.title: Real-World: Temperature Conversion
exercises.description: Create a variable `celsius = 25`, then calculate and store `fahrenheit` using the formula: F = (C × 9/5) + 32.
hints: The formula is: fahrenheit = (celsius * 9/5) + 32
25°C should be 77°F
exercises.solution:
celsius = 25
fahrenheit = (celsius * 9/5) + 32
exercises.test_code:
assert celsius == 25, f"celsius should be 25, got {celsius!r}"
assert fahrenheit == 77.0, f"fahrenheit should be 77.0, got {fahrenheit!r}"

exercises.title: String Methods on Variables
exercises.description: Create a variable `text = "python"` and use string methods to create `upper_text` (uppercase) and `title_text` (title case).
hints: String methods: .upper(), .title(), .lower()
Example: upper_text = text.upper()
exercises.solution:
text = "python"
upper_text = text.upper()
title_text = text.title()
exercises.starter_code:
text = "python"
# Create uppercase and title case versions
exercises.test_code:
assert text == 'python', f"text should be 'python', got {text!r}"
assert upper_text == 'PYTHON', f"upper_text should be 'PYTHON', got {upper_text!r}"
assert title_text == 'Python', f"title_text should be 'Python', got {title_text!r}"

exercises.title: List Variable with Multiple Elements
exercises.description: Create a list variable `fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]` and access the first and last elements.
hints: Lists use square brackets: [element1, element2, ...]
Index 0 is first, -1 is last
exercises.solution:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
first = fruits[0]
last = fruits[-1]
exercises.test_code:
assert fruits == ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'], f"fruits has wrong value"
assert first == 'apple', f"first should be 'apple', got {first!r}"
assert last == 'cherry', f"last should be 'cherry', got {last!r}"

exercises.title: Dictionary Variable Access
exercises.description: Create a dictionary `person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "NYC"}` and access the values for name and age.
hints: Dictionaries use curly braces: {key: value, ...}
Access values: dict_var["key"]
exercises.test_code:
assert person == {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30, 'city': 'NYC'}, f"person has wrong value"
assert name == 'Alice', f"name should be 'Alice', got {name!r}"
assert age == 30, f"age should be 30, got {age!r}"

exercises.title: Variable Scope: Global vs Local
exercises.description: Create a global variable `global_var = 10`, then create a function that creates a local variable `local_var = 20` and returns their sum.
hints: Global variables exist outside functions
Local variables exist only inside functions
exercises.solution:
global_var = 10
def add_vars():
    local_var = 20
    return global_var + local_var
result = add_vars()
exercises.starter_code:
global_var = 10
def add_vars():
    # Create local variable and sum with global
    pass
exercises.test_code:
assert 'add_vars' in dir(), "Function 'add_vars' not defined. Did you write: def add_vars(...)?"
_r0 = add_vars()
assert _r0 == 30, f'Test 1: add_vars() returned {_r0!r}, expected 30'

exercises.title: Variable Shadowing
exercises.description: Create a global variable `x = 5`, then create a function that creates a local variable `x = 10` and returns it (shadowing the global).
hints: Shadowing occurs when a local variable has the same name as a global
Inside the function, the local x 'shadows' the global x
exercises.solution:
x = 5
def shadow_test():
    x = 10
    return x
result = shadow_test()
exercises.starter_code:
x = 5
def shadow_test():
    # Create local x that shadows global
    pass
exercises.test_code:
assert 'shadow_test' in dir(), "Function 'shadow_test' not defined. Did you write: def shadow_test(...)?"
_r0 = shadow_test()
assert _r0 == 10, f'Test 1: shadow_test() returned {_r0!r}, expected 10'

exercises.title: Augmented Assignment with Strings
exercises.description: Start with `text = "Hello"`, then use += to append " World" to it.
hints: The += operator works with strings to concatenate
text += " World" appends to the existing string
exercises.solution:
text = "Hello"
text += " World"
exercises.test_code: assert text == 'Hello World', f"text should be 'Hello World', got {text!r}"

exercises.title: Multiple Operations with Variables
exercises.description: Create variables for a store: `item_price = 25`, `quantity = 4`, then calculate `subtotal`, then add `discount = 0.1` (10% off), and store final `price`.
hints: subtotal = item_price * quantity
discount_amount = subtotal * discount; price = subtotal - discount_amount
exercises.solution:
item_price = 25
quantity = 4
subtotal = item_price * quantity
discount = 0.1
discount_amount = subtotal * discount
price = subtotal - discount_amount
exercises.test_code:
assert item_price == 25, f"item_price should be 25, got {item_price!r}"
assert quantity == 4, f"quantity should be 4, got {quantity!r}"
assert subtotal == 100, f"subtotal should be 100, got {subtotal!r}"
assert discount == 0.1, f"discount should be 0.1, got {discount!r}"
assert discount_amount == 10.0, f"discount_amount should be 10.0, got {discount_amount!r}"
assert price == 90.0, f"price should be 90.0, got {price!r}"

exercises.title: Dynamic Type Change
exercises.description: Create a variable `value = "123"` (string), then reassign it to the integer 123, showing Python's dynamic typing.
hints: Python allows a variable to change types
Use int(value) to convert or reassign directly
exercises.solution:
value = "123"
value = int(value)
exercises.test_code: assert value == 123, f"value should be 123, got {value!r}"

exercises.title: Boolean Expression Result
exercises.description: Create variables `a = 10` and `b = 5`, then create a boolean variable `is_greater = a > b` that stores the result of the comparison.
hints: Comparison operators: >, <, ==, !=, >=, <=
is_greater = a > b will be True
exercises.solution:
a = 10
b = 5
is_greater = a > b
exercises.starter_code:
a = 10
b = 5
# Create boolean variable from comparison
exercises.test_code:
assert a == 10, f"a should be 10, got {a!r}"
assert b == 5, f"b should be 5, got {b!r}"
assert is_greater == True, f"is_greater should be True, got {is_greater!r}"

exercises.title: F-String with Expressions
exercises.description: Create variables `width = 10` and `height = 20`, then use an f-string to display 'Area: [calculated area]'.
hints: F-strings can contain expressions inside curly braces
f"Area: {width * height}"
exercises.solution:
width = 10
height = 20
area_message = f"Area: {width * height}"
exercises.starter_code:
width = 10
height = 20
# Use f-string with calculation
exercises.test_code:
assert width == 10, f"width should be 10, got {width!r}"
assert height == 20, f"height should be 20, got {height!r}"
assert area_message == 'Area: 200', f"area_message should be 'Area: 200', got {area_message!r}"

exercises.title: Tuple Unpacking with Rest
exercises.description: Unpack the tuple (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) where `first = 1`, `second = 2`, and `rest = [3, 4, 5]` using * unpacking.
hints: Use the * operator to capture remaining elements
first, second, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
exercises.solution: first, second, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
exercises.test_code:
assert first == 1, f"first should be 1, got {first!r}"
assert second == 2, f"second should be 2, got {second!r}"
assert rest == [3, 4, 5], f"rest has wrong value"

exercises.title: Variable Swap Advanced
exercises.description: Given three variables `a = 1`, `b = 2`, `c = 3`, rotate their values so that a=2, b=3, c=1.
hints: Multiple assignment allows rotation: a, b, c = b, c, a
This shifts each value one position
exercises.solution:
a, b, c = 1, 2, 3
a, b, c = b, c, a
exercises.test_code:
assert a == 2, f"a should be 2, got {a!r}"
assert b == 3, f"b should be 3, got {b!r}"
assert c == 1, f"c should be 1, got {c!r}"

exercises.title: Real-World: User Profile
exercises.description: Create a user profile with variables: `first_name`, `last_name`, `email`, `age`, then use an f-string to create a profile summary.
hints: Combine multiple variables of different types
Use f-string to format: f"User: {first_name} {last_name}, Email: {email}, Age: {age}"
exercises.solution:
first_name = "John"
last_name = "Smith"
email = "john@example.com"
age = 28
profile = f"User: {first_name} {last_name}, Email: {email}, Age: {age}"
exercises.test_code:
assert first_name == 'John', f"first_name should be 'John', got {first_name!r}"
assert last_name == 'Smith', f"last_name should be 'Smith', got {last_name!r}"
assert email == 'john@example.com', f"email should be 'john@example.com', got {email!r}"
assert age == 28, f"age should be 28, got {age!r}"
assert profile == 'User: John Smith, Email: john@example.com, Age: 28', f"profile should be 'User: John Smith, Email: john@example.com, Age: 28', got {profile!r}"

exercises.title: Real-World: BMI Calculator
exercises.description: Calculate BMI with variables: `weight_kg = 70`, `height_m = 1.75`. BMI = weight / (height²). Store in `bmi` variable.
hints: BMI formula: bmi = weight_kg / (height_m ** 2)
Result should be approximately 22.86
exercises.solution:
weight_kg = 70
height_m = 1.75
bmi = weight_kg / (height_m ** 2)
exercises.test_code:
assert weight_kg == 70, f"weight_kg should be 70, got {weight_kg!r}"
assert height_m == 1.75, f"height_m should be 1.75, got {height_m!r}"
assert bmi == 22.857142857142858, f"bmi should be 22.857142857142858, got {bmi!r}"

exercises.title: Constants and Reuse
exercises.description: Define mathematical constants `PI = 3.14159` and `E = 2.71828`, then use them to calculate `circle_area = PI * 5**2` and `exponential_e = E**2`.
hints: Define constants in ALL_CAPS
Reuse them in multiple calculations
exercises.solution:
PI = 3.14159
E = 2.71828
circle_area = PI * 5**2
exponential_e = E**2
exercises.test_code:
assert PI == 3.14159, f"PI should be 3.14159, got {PI!r}"
assert E == 2.71828, f"E should be 2.71828, got {E!r}"
assert circle_area == 78.53975, f"circle_area should be 78.53975, got {circle_area!r}"
assert exponential_e == 7.3890461584, f"exponential_e should be 7.3890461584, got {exponential_e!r}"

exercises.title: List Comprehension Assignment
exercises.description: Create a variable `squares` that stores squares of numbers 1-5 using list comprehension: [1, 4, 9, 16, 25].
hints: List comprehension syntax: [expression for item in iterable]
[x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
exercises.solution: squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
exercises.starter_code: # Create list of squares using comprehension
exercises.test_code: assert squares == [1, 4, 9, 16, 25], f"squares has wrong value"

exercises.title: Dictionary from Variables
exercises.description: Create variables `keys = ["a", "b", "c"]` and `values = [1, 2, 3]`, then create a dictionary `my_dict = {"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}` using dict() or dict comprehension.
hints: Use zip() to pair keys and values: dict(zip(keys, values))
Or use dict comprehension: {k: v for k, v in zip(keys, values)}
exercises.solution:
keys = ["a", "b", "c"]
values = [1, 2, 3]
my_dict = dict(zip(keys, values))
exercises.test_code:
assert keys == ['a', 'b', 'c'], f"keys has wrong value"
assert values == [1, 2, 3], f"values has wrong value"
assert my_dict == {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, f"my_dict has wrong value"

exercises.title: Mutable vs Immutable: List Assignment
exercises.description: Create a list `original = [1, 2, 3]`, assign it to `copy = original`, then modify copy to [1, 2, 3, 4]. Show that both reference the same list.
hints: Lists are mutable and share references
copy = original doesn't make a new list, it makes another reference
exercises.solution:
original = [1, 2, 3]
copy = original
copy.append(4)
same_reference = original is copy
exercises.starter_code:
original = [1, 2, 3]
copy = original
# Modify and check
exercises.test_code:
assert original == [1, 2, 3, 4], f"original has wrong value"
assert copy == [1, 2, 3, 4], f"copy has wrong value"
assert same_reference == True, f"same_reference should be True, got {same_reference!r}"

exercises.title: Mutable vs Immutable: String Assignment
exercises.description: Create a string `original = "hello"`, assign it to `copy = original`, then try to modify copy (strings are immutable, so create a new string).
hints: Strings are immutable, so reassignment creates a new object
copy = original + " world" creates a new string
exercises.solution:
original = "hello"
copy = original
copy = copy + " world"
same_reference = original is copy
exercises.starter_code:
original = "hello"
copy = original
# Modify and check
exercises.test_code:
assert original == 'hello', f"original should be 'hello', got {original!r}"
assert copy == 'hello world', f"copy should be 'hello world', got {copy!r}"
assert same_reference == False, f"same_reference should be False, got {same_reference!r}"

exercises.title: Nested Data Structure Assignment
exercises.description: Create a nested dictionary `student = {"name": "Alice", "grades": {"math": 95, "english": 88}}` and access nested values.
hints: Nested structures: student["grades"]["math"]
Dictionaries inside dictionaries
exercises.test_code:
assert student == {'name': 'Alice', 'grades': {'math': 95, 'english': 88}}, f"student has wrong value"
assert math_grade == 95, f"math_grade should be 95, got {math_grade!r}"
assert english_grade == 88, f"english_grade should be 88, got {english_grade!r}"

exercises.title: Variable with Conditional Expression
exercises.description: Create a variable `score = 85`, then use a conditional expression to assign `grade = "A" if score >= 90 else "B"` (ternary operator).
hints: Ternary conditional: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false
grade = "A" if score >= 90 else "B"
exercises.solution:
score = 85
grade = "A" if score >= 90 else "B"
exercises.test_code:
assert score == 85, f"score should be 85, got {score!r}"
assert grade == 'B', f"grade should be 'B', got {grade!r}"

exercises.title: Generator Expression Assignment
exercises.description: Create a generator `even_numbers = (x for x in range(1, 11) if x % 2 == 0)` that yields even numbers 1-10.
hints: Generator comprehension uses parentheses: (expression for ...)
Similar to list comprehension but lazy (doesn't compute all at once)
exercises.solution: even_numbers = (x for x in range(1, 11) if x % 2 == 0)
exercises.test_code: pass  # Verify your code runs without errors

exercises.title: Real-World: Invoice with Multiple Variables
exercises.description: Create an invoice: `items = [("Widget", 10, 5), ("Gadget", 15, 2)]` (name, price, qty), calculate `total`, add `tax = 0.08`, and final `invoice_total`.
hints: items is a list of tuples
total = sum(price * qty for each item); invoice_total = total * (1 + tax)
exercises.solution:
items = [("Widget", 10, 5), ("Gadget", 15, 2)]
total = sum(price * qty for name, price, qty in items)
tax = 0.08
invoice_total = total * (1 + tax)
exercises.test_code:
assert items == [('Widget', 10, 5), ('Gadget', 15, 2)], f"items has wrong value"
assert total == 80, f"total should be 80, got {total!r}"
assert tax == 0.08, f"tax should be 0.08, got {tax!r}"
assert invoice_total == 86.4, f"invoice_total should be 86.4, got {invoice_total!r}"

exercises.title: Real-World: Weather Data Analysis
exercises.description: Store daily temps: `temps = [72, 75, 68, 70, 73]`, then calculate `avg_temp`, `max_temp`, `min_temp`, and create a summary f-string.
hints: Use sum(), len(), max(), min() functions

exercises.solution:
temps = [72, 75, 68, 70, 73]
avg_temp = sum(temps) / len(temps)
max_temp = max(temps)

exercises.test_code:

exercises.title: Global Keyword and Modification
exercises.description: Create a global variable `counter = 0`, then create a function that uses the `global` keyword to increment it.
hints: Use `global variable_name` inside a function to modify global scope
Without global, assignment creates a local variable
exercises.solution:
counter = 0
def increment():
    global counter
    counter += 1
    return counter
exercises.starter_code:
counter = 0
def increment():
    # Use global to modify counter
    pass
exercises.test_code:
assert 'increment' in dir(), "Function 'increment' not defined. Did you write: def increment(...)?"
_r0 = increment()
assert _r0 == 1, f'Test 1: increment() returned {_r0!r}, expected 1'

exercises.description: Create a function `celsius_to_fahrenheit(c)` that converts Celsius to Fahrenheit using the formula F = C * 9/5 + 32.
hints: Use the exact formula: (celsius * 9/5) + 32

exercises.title: Validate Email Format
exercises.description: Create a function `is_valid_email(email)` that returns True if the string contains exactly one '@' and at least one '.' after the '@'.
hints: Use string methods like count() and find()
Check that '@' appears exactly once and '.' appears after it
exercises.solution:
def is_valid_email(email):
    return email.count('@') == 1 and '.' in email.split('@')[1]
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_valid_email' in dir(), "Function 'is_valid_email' not defined. Did you write: def is_valid_email(...)?"
_r0 = is_valid_email('test@example.com')
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_valid_email('test@example.com') returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_valid_email('invalid@email')
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: is_valid_email('invalid@email') returned {_r1!r}, expected False'
_r2 = is_valid_email('test@@@example.com')
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: is_valid_email('test@@@example.com') returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = is_valid_email('notanemail.com')
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: is_valid_email('notanemail.com') returned {_r3!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Reverse a String
exercises.description: Create a function `reverse_string(s)` that returns the string reversed using slicing.
hints: Use slice notation with negative step: s[::-1]
'hello' reversed is 'olleh'
exercises.solution:
def reverse_string(s):
    return s[::-1]
exercises.test_code:
assert 'reverse_string' in dir(), "Function 'reverse_string' not defined. Did you write: def reverse_string(...)?"
_r0 = reverse_string('hello')
assert _r0 == 'olleh', f'Test 1: reverse_string('hello') returned {_r0!r}, expected 'olleh''
_r1 = reverse_string('Python')
assert _r1 == 'nohtyP', f'Test 2: reverse_string('Python') returned {_r1!r}, expected 'nohtyP''
_r2 = reverse_string('a')
assert _r2 == 'a', f'Test 3: reverse_string('a') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'a''

exercises.title: Count Vowels in String
exercises.description: Create a function `count_vowels(text)` that returns the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in the text (case-insensitive).
hints: Convert to lowercase first with .lower()
Loop through characters and count those in 'aeiou'
exercises.solution:
def count_vowels(text):
    return sum(1 for c in text.lower() if c in 'aeiou')
exercises.test_code:
assert 'count_vowels' in dir(), "Function 'count_vowels' not defined. Did you write: def count_vowels(...)?"
_r0 = count_vowels('Hello')
assert _r0 == 2, f'Test 1: count_vowels('Hello') returned {_r0!r}, expected 2'
_r1 = count_vowels('AEIOUaeiou')
assert _r1 == 10, f'Test 2: count_vowels('AEIOUaeiou') returned {_r1!r}, expected 10'
_r2 = count_vowels('xyz')
assert _r2 == 0, f'Test 3: count_vowels('xyz') returned {_r2!r}, expected 0'

exercises.title: Parse Integer from String with Error Handling
exercises.description: Create a function `safe_int(value)` that converts a string to int, returning -1 if conversion fails.
hints: Use try/except to catch ValueError
Return the converted integer or -1 on error
exercises.test_code:
assert 'safe_int' in dir(), "Function 'safe_int' not defined. Did you write: def safe_int(...)?"
_r0 = safe_int('123')
assert _r0 == 123, f'Test 1: safe_int('123') returned {_r0!r}, expected 123'
_r1 = safe_int('abc')
assert _r1 == -1, f'Test 2: safe_int('abc') returned {_r1!r}, expected -1'
_r2 = safe_int('45.6')
assert _r2 == -1, f'Test 3: safe_int('45.6') returned {_r2!r}, expected -1'

exercises.title: Truncate Float to N Decimals
exercises.description: Create a function `truncate_float(value, decimals)` that truncates (not rounds) a float to N decimal places.
hints: Use int() and multiplication: int(value * 10**decimals) / 10**decimals
3.14159 truncated to 2 places is 3.14, not 3.15
exercises.test_code:
assert 'truncate_float' in dir(), "Function 'truncate_float' not defined. Did you write: def truncate_float(...)?"
_r0 = truncate_float(3.14159, 2)
assert _r0 == 3.14, f'Test 1: truncate_float(3.14159, 2) returned {_r0!r}, expected 3.14'
_r1 = truncate_float(99.9999, 1)
assert _r1 == 99.9, f'Test 2: truncate_float(99.9999, 1) returned {_r1!r}, expected 99.9'
_r2 = truncate_float(5, 2)
assert _r2 == 5.0, f'Test 3: truncate_float(5, 2) returned {_r2!r}, expected 5.0'

exercises.title: Check if String is Numeric
exercises.description: Create a function `is_numeric(s)` that returns True if the string can be converted to a float.
hints: Try converting with float() and catch ValueError
Handle both integers and floats: '123' and '3.14' should both return True
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_numeric' in dir(), "Function 'is_numeric' not defined. Did you write: def is_numeric(...)?"
_r0 = is_numeric('123')
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_numeric('123') returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_numeric('3.14')
assert _r1 == True, f'Test 2: is_numeric('3.14') returned {_r1!r}, expected True'
_r2 = is_numeric('abc')
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: is_numeric('abc') returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = is_numeric('12.34.56')
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: is_numeric('12.34.56') returned {_r3!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Extract Numbers from String
exercises.description: Create a function `extract_digits(text)` that returns a string containing only the digits from the input.
hints: Use a loop or generator to filter characters
Check if c.isdigit() for each character
exercises.solution:
def extract_digits(text):
    return ''.join(c for c in text if c.isdigit())
exercises.test_code:
assert 'extract_digits' in dir(), "Function 'extract_digits' not defined. Did you write: def extract_digits(...)?"
_r0 = extract_digits('abc123def456')
assert _r0 == '123456', f'Test 1: extract_digits('abc123def456') returned {_r0!r}, expected '123456''
_r1 = extract_digits('no digits here')
assert _r1 == '', f'Test 2: extract_digits('no digits here') returned {_r1!r}, expected '''
_r2 = extract_digits('2024-04-08')
assert _r2 == '20240408', f'Test 3: extract_digits('2024-04-08') returned {_r2!r}, expected '20240408''

exercises.title: Capitalize First Letter Only
exercises.description: Create a function `capitalize_first(text)` that capitalizes only the first character, leaving the rest unchanged.
hints: Use indexing and slicing: text[0].upper() + text[1:]
Handle empty strings gracefully
exercises.solution:
def capitalize_first(text):
    return text[0].upper() + text[1:] if text else text
exercises.test_code:
assert 'capitalize_first' in dir(), "Function 'capitalize_first' not defined. Did you write: def capitalize_first(...)?"
_r0 = capitalize_first('hello world')
assert _r0 == 'Hello world', f'Test 1: capitalize_first('hello world') returned {_r0!r}, expected 'Hello world''
_r1 = capitalize_first('HELLO')
assert _r1 == 'HELLO', f'Test 2: capitalize_first('HELLO') returned {_r1!r}, expected 'HELLO''
_r2 = capitalize_first('a')
assert _r2 == 'A', f'Test 3: capitalize_first('a') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'A''

exercises.title: Format Large Numbers with Commas
exercises.description: Create a function `format_number(n)` that formats an integer with thousand separators, e.g., 1000000 becomes '1,000,000'.
hints: Use Python's built-in format() function or string method
format(1000000, ',') works, or use f'{n:,}'
exercises.solution:
def format_number(n):
    return f'{n:,}'
exercises.test_code:
assert 'format_number' in dir(), "Function 'format_number' not defined. Did you write: def format_number(...)?"
_r0 = format_number(1000)
assert _r0 == '1,000', f'Test 1: format_number(1000) returned {_r0!r}, expected '1,000''
_r1 = format_number(1000000)
assert _r1 == '1,000,000', f'Test 2: format_number(1000000) returned {_r1!r}, expected '1,000,000''
_r2 = format_number(42)
assert _r2 == '42', f'Test 3: format_number(42) returned {_r2!r}, expected '42''

exercises.title: Build Query String from Dictionary
exercises.description: Create a function `build_query_string(params)` that takes a dictionary and returns a URL query string, e.g., {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30} becomes 'name=Alice&age=30'.
hints: Iterate through dictionary items
Join with '&' separator and use '=' between key and value
exercises.solution:
def build_query_string(params):
    return '&'.join(f'{k}={v}' for k, v in params.items())
exercises.test_code:
assert 'build_query_string' in dir(), "Function 'build_query_string' not defined. Did you write: def build_query_string(...)?"
_r0 = build_query_string({'name': 'Alice', 'age': '30'})
assert _r0 == 'name=Alice&age=30', f'Test 1: build_query_string({'name': 'Alice', 'age': '30'}) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'name=Alice&age=30''
_r1 = build_query_string({'x': '1'})
assert _r1 == 'x=1', f'Test 2: build_query_string({'x': '1'}) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'x=1''

exercises.title: Type-Based Processing
exercises.description: Create a function `describe_value(val)` that returns a description string based on the type: 'int' for int, 'float' for float, 'str' for str, or 'other' for anything else.
hints: Use type() or isinstance() to check types
Return appropriate strings based on the type
exercises.test_code:
assert 'describe_value' in dir(), "Function 'describe_value' not defined. Did you write: def describe_value(...)?"
_r0 = describe_value(42)
assert _r0 == 'int', f'Test 1: describe_value(42) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'int''
_r1 = describe_value(3.14)
assert _r1 == 'float', f'Test 2: describe_value(3.14) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'float''
_r2 = describe_value('hello')
assert _r2 == 'str', f'Test 3: describe_value('hello') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'str''
_r3 = describe_value(True)
assert _r3 == 'bool', f'Test 4: describe_value(True) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'bool''
_r4 = describe_value([])
assert _r4 == 'other', f'Test 5: describe_value([]) returned {_r4!r}, expected 'other''

exercises.title: Parse CSV Line into Typed Values
exercises.description: Create a function `parse_csv_line(line, types)` that splits a CSV line and converts each field to the specified type. E.g., parse_csv_line('1,3.14,hello', [int, float, str]) returns [1, 3.14, 'hello'].
hints: Use split(',') to split the line
Use a list comprehension to convert each value with its type
handle the conversion: type(value_string)
exercises.test_code:
assert 'parse_csv_line' in dir(), "Function 'parse_csv_line' not defined. Did you write: def parse_csv_line(...)?"
_r0 = parse_csv_line('1,3.14,hello', [<class 'int'>, <class 'float'>, <class 'str'>])
assert _r0 == [1, 3.14, 'hello'], f'Test 1: parse_csv_line('1,3.14,hello', [<class 'int'>, <class 'float'>, <class 'str'>]) returned {_r0!r}, expected [1, 3.14, 'hello']'
_r1 = parse_csv_line('42,true,test', [<class 'int'>, <class 'str'>, <class 'str'>])
assert _r1 == [42, 'true', 'test'], f'Test 2: parse_csv_line('42,true,test', [<class 'int'>, <class 'str'>, <class 'str'>]) returned {_r1!r}, expected [42, 'true', 'test']'

exercises.title: Robust Type Conversion Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a function `safe_convert(value, target_type)` that attempts to convert value to target_type, returning None on any error. It should handle int, float, str, and bool types.
hints: Use try/except for each type conversion
For bool, convert non-empty strings and non-zero numbers to True
Return None if conversion fails
exercises.test_code:
assert 'safe_convert' in dir(), "Function 'safe_convert' not defined. Did you write: def safe_convert(...)?"
_r0 = safe_convert('123', <class 'int'>)
assert _r0 == 123, f'Test 1: safe_convert('123', <class 'int'>) returned {_r0!r}, expected 123'
_r1 = safe_convert('3.14', <class 'float'>)
assert _r1 == 3.14, f'Test 2: safe_convert('3.14', <class 'float'>) returned {_r1!r}, expected 3.14'
_r2 = safe_convert('hello', <class 'str'>)
assert _r2 == 'hello', f'Test 3: safe_convert('hello', <class 'str'>) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'hello''
_r3 = safe_convert('abc', <class 'int'>)
assert _r3 == None, f'Test 4: safe_convert('abc', <class 'int'>) returned {_r3!r}, expected None'
_r4 = safe_convert('true', <class 'bool'>)
assert _r4 == True, f'Test 5: safe_convert('true', <class 'bool'>) returned {_r4!r}, expected True'

exercises.title: Simple if statement
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a number is positive, False otherwise.
hints: Use the > operator to compare with 0
Return the result of the comparison directly
exercises.solution:
def is_positive(n):
    return n > 0
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_positive' in dir(), "Function 'is_positive' not defined. Did you write: def is_positive(...)?"
_r0 = is_positive(5)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_positive(5) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_positive(-3)
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: is_positive(-3) returned {_r1!r}, expected False'
_r2 = is_positive(0)
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: is_positive(0) returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = is_positive(100)
assert _r3 == True, f'Test 4: is_positive(100) returned {_r3!r}, expected True'

exercises.title: if/else with equality
exercises.description: Write a function that returns 'even' if a number is even, 'odd' if odd.
hints: Use the modulo operator % to check remainder when divided by 2
n % 2 == 0 means the number is even
exercises.test_code:
assert 'even_or_odd' in dir(), "Function 'even_or_odd' not defined. Did you write: def even_or_odd(...)?"
_r0 = even_or_odd(4)
assert _r0 == 'even', f'Test 1: even_or_odd(4) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'even''
_r1 = even_or_odd(7)
assert _r1 == 'odd', f'Test 2: even_or_odd(7) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'odd''
_r2 = even_or_odd(0)
assert _r2 == 'even', f'Test 3: even_or_odd(0) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'even''
_r3 = even_or_odd(13)
assert _r3 == 'odd', f'Test 4: even_or_odd(13) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'odd''

exercises.title: Comparison: less than and greater than
exercises.description: Write a function that returns 'hot' if temperature >= 30, 'cold' if < 10, 'mild' otherwise.
hints: Use >= for greater-than-or-equal
Use < for less-than
Consider order of conditions carefully
exercises.test_code:
assert 'temperature_description' in dir(), "Function 'temperature_description' not defined. Did you write: def temperature_description(...)?"
_r0 = temperature_description(35)
assert _r0 == 'hot', f'Test 1: temperature_description(35) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'hot''
_r1 = temperature_description(5)
assert _r1 == 'cold', f'Test 2: temperature_description(5) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'cold''
_r2 = temperature_description(20)
assert _r2 == 'mild', f'Test 3: temperature_description(20) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'mild''
_r3 = temperature_description(30)
assert _r3 == 'hot', f'Test 4: temperature_description(30) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'hot''
_r4 = temperature_description(9)
assert _r4 == 'cold', f'Test 5: temperature_description(9) returned {_r4!r}, expected 'cold''

exercises.title: Inequality operator !=
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a string is not 'admin', False otherwise.
hints: Use the != operator for 'not equal'
String comparison with == and != is case-sensitive
exercises.solution:
def is_not_admin(username):
    return username != 'admin'
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_not_admin' in dir(), "Function 'is_not_admin' not defined. Did you write: def is_not_admin(...)?"
_r0 = is_not_admin('user')
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_not_admin('user') returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_not_admin('admin')
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: is_not_admin('admin') returned {_r1!r}, expected False'
_r2 = is_not_admin('Admin')
assert _r2 == True, f'Test 3: is_not_admin('Admin') returned {_r2!r}, expected True'
_r3 = is_not_admin('guest')
assert _r3 == True, f'Test 4: is_not_admin('guest') returned {_r3!r}, expected True'

exercises.title: if/elif/else chain with multiple conditions
exercises.description: Write a function that returns a size category: 'small' (< 5), 'medium' (5-10), 'large' (> 10).
hints: Use < for less than and > for greater than
elif handles the middle condition
Order matters: check small first, then medium, then large
exercises.test_code:
assert 'size_category' in dir(), "Function 'size_category' not defined. Did you write: def size_category(...)?"
_r0 = size_category(2)
assert _r0 == 'small', f'Test 1: size_category(2) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'small''
_r1 = size_category(5)
assert _r1 == 'medium', f'Test 2: size_category(5) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'medium''
_r2 = size_category(10)
assert _r2 == 'medium', f'Test 3: size_category(10) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'medium''
_r3 = size_category(15)
assert _r3 == 'large', f'Test 4: size_category(15) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'large''

exercises.title: Boolean return from comparison
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a number is between 1 and 100 inclusive, False otherwise.
hints: You need to check two conditions: >= 1 AND <= 100
You can use 'and' or write two separate comparisons
Return the boolean result directly
exercises.solution:
def is_in_range(n):
    return 1 <= n <= 100
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_in_range' in dir(), "Function 'is_in_range' not defined. Did you write: def is_in_range(...)?"
_r0 = is_in_range(50)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_in_range(50) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_in_range(1)
assert _r1 == True, f'Test 2: is_in_range(1) returned {_r1!r}, expected True'
_r2 = is_in_range(100)
assert _r2 == True, f'Test 3: is_in_range(100) returned {_r2!r}, expected True'
_r3 = is_in_range(0)
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: is_in_range(0) returned {_r3!r}, expected False'
_r4 = is_in_range(101)
assert _r4 == False, f'Test 5: is_in_range(101) returned {_r4!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Basic logical AND
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a number is even AND positive, False otherwise.
hints: Use 'and' to combine two conditions
Check n > 0 and n % 2 == 0
Both conditions must be True for the result to be True
exercises.solution:
def even_and_positive(n):
    return n > 0 and n % 2 == 0
exercises.test_code:
assert 'even_and_positive' in dir(), "Function 'even_and_positive' not defined. Did you write: def even_and_positive(...)?"
_r0 = even_and_positive(4)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: even_and_positive(4) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = even_and_positive(2)
assert _r1 == True, f'Test 2: even_and_positive(2) returned {_r1!r}, expected True'
_r2 = even_and_positive(-2)
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: even_and_positive(-2) returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = even_and_positive(3)
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: even_and_positive(3) returned {_r3!r}, expected False'
_r4 = even_and_positive(0)
assert _r4 == False, f'Test 5: even_and_positive(0) returned {_r4!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Basic logical OR
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a number is less than 0 OR greater than 100, False otherwise.
hints: Use 'or' to combine two conditions
Check n < 0 or n > 100
At least one condition needs to be True
exercises.solution:
def outside_range(n):
    return n < 0 or n > 100
exercises.test_code:
assert 'outside_range' in dir(), "Function 'outside_range' not defined. Did you write: def outside_range(...)?"
_r0 = outside_range(-5)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: outside_range(-5) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = outside_range(150)
assert _r1 == True, f'Test 2: outside_range(150) returned {_r1!r}, expected True'
_r2 = outside_range(50)
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: outside_range(50) returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = outside_range(0)
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: outside_range(0) returned {_r3!r}, expected False'
_r4 = outside_range(100)
assert _r4 == False, f'Test 5: outside_range(100) returned {_r4!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: NOT operator
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a string is empty (length 0), False otherwise.
hints: Use 'not' to negate a boolean
len(s) == 0 checks if string is empty
You could also use: not s
exercises.solution:
def is_empty(s):
    return len(s) == 0
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_empty' in dir(), "Function 'is_empty' not defined. Did you write: def is_empty(...)?"
_r0 = is_empty('')
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_empty('') returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_empty('hello')
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: is_empty('hello') returned {_r1!r}, expected False'
_r2 = is_empty(' ')
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: is_empty(' ') returned {_r2!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Truthiness of values
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a value is truthy (0, empty string, None, False are falsy), False if falsy.
hints: Empty collections, 0, None, and False are falsy
Non-zero numbers and non-empty strings are truthy
Use 'if value:' to check truthiness
exercises.solution:
def is_truthy(value):
    return bool(value)
exercises.test_code:
assert 'is_truthy' in dir(), "Function 'is_truthy' not defined. Did you write: def is_truthy(...)?"
_r0 = is_truthy(1)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: is_truthy(1) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = is_truthy(0)
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: is_truthy(0) returned {_r1!r}, expected False'
_r2 = is_truthy('hello')
assert _r2 == True, f'Test 3: is_truthy('hello') returned {_r2!r}, expected True'
_r3 = is_truthy('')
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: is_truthy('') returned {_r3!r}, expected False'
_r4 = is_truthy(None)
assert _r4 == False, f'Test 5: is_truthy(None) returned {_r4!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Nested if statements
exercises.description: Write a function that returns 'adult' if age >= 18 and has_id is True, 'minor' otherwise.
hints: Use a nested if: first check age, then check has_id inside
Both conditions must be True to return 'adult'
Remember boolean parameters are either True or False
exercises.test_code:
assert 'check_adult' in dir(), "Function 'check_adult' not defined. Did you write: def check_adult(...)?"
_r0 = check_adult(25, True)
assert _r0 == 'adult', f'Test 1: check_adult(25, True) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'adult''
_r1 = check_adult(25, False)
assert _r1 == 'minor', f'Test 2: check_adult(25, False) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'minor''
_r2 = check_adult(16, True)
assert _r2 == 'minor', f'Test 3: check_adult(16, True) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'minor''
_r3 = check_adult(18, True)
assert _r3 == 'adult', f'Test 4: check_adult(18, True) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'adult''

exercises.title: Ternary expression (inline if/else)
exercises.description: Write a function that returns 'pass' if score >= 50 else 'fail' using a ternary expression.
hints: Use: x if condition else y syntax
This is a one-liner alternative to if/else
Read it as: 'x (if condition is true) else y'
exercises.solution:
def pass_or_fail(score):
    return 'pass' if score >= 50 else 'fail'
exercises.test_code:
assert 'pass_or_fail' in dir(), "Function 'pass_or_fail' not defined. Did you write: def pass_or_fail(...)?"
_r0 = pass_or_fail(50)
assert _r0 == 'pass', f'Test 1: pass_or_fail(50) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'pass''
_r1 = pass_or_fail(75)
assert _r1 == 'pass', f'Test 2: pass_or_fail(75) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'pass''
_r2 = pass_or_fail(30)
assert _r2 == 'fail', f'Test 3: pass_or_fail(30) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'fail''
_r3 = pass_or_fail(49)
assert _r3 == 'fail', f'Test 4: pass_or_fail(49) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'fail''

exercises.title: Chained comparisons
exercises.description: Write a function that returns True if a number is strictly between 10 and 20 (exclusive), False otherwise.
hints: Use chained comparison: 10 < n < 20
This is more readable than (n > 10 and n < 20)
Works for any number of comparisons
exercises.solution:
def is_between_10_and_20(n):
    return 10 < n < 20
exercises.test_code:

exercises.title: Complex boolean logic with AND/OR/NOT

hints: Use 'and' and 'or' operators

Remember: 'and' has higher precedence than 'or'
exercises.solution:
def can_drive(age, has_license, in_driving_school):
    return (age >= 18 and has_license) or (age >= 16 and in_driving_school)

exercises.title: Short-circuit evaluation
exercises.description: Write a function that checks if a list is non-empty AND its first element is positive, without IndexError.

exercises.solution:
def first_is_positive(lst):
    return len(lst) > 0 and lst[0] > 0

Parentheses: (A and B and C) structure
exercises.solution:
def loan_eligible(age, income, has_collateral, credit_score):
    return (age >= 21 and 
            (income >= 30000 or has_collateral) and 
            credit_score >= 650)
exercises.test_code:
assert 'loan_eligible' in dir(), "Function 'loan_eligible' not defined. Did you write: def loan_eligible(...)?"
_r0 = loan_eligible(25, 35000, False, 700)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: loan_eligible(25, 35000, False, 700) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = loan_eligible(25, 25000, True, 700)
assert _r1 == True, f'Test 2: loan_eligible(25, 25000, True, 700) returned {_r1!r}, expected True'
_r2 = loan_eligible(20, 35000, False, 700)
assert _r2 == False, f'Test 3: loan_eligible(20, 35000, False, 700) returned {_r2!r}, expected False'
_r3 = loan_eligible(25, 25000, False, 600)
assert _r3 == False, f'Test 4: loan_eligible(25, 25000, False, 600) returned {_r3!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Game rock-paper-scissors
exercises.description: Write a function that takes player1 and player2 moves and returns 'player1', 'player2', or 'tie'.
hints: Rock beats scissors, scissors beats paper, paper beats rock
Use if/elif chain for outcomes
Handle tie case separately
exercises.solution:
def rps_winner(p1, p2):
    if p1 == p2:
        return 'tie'
    if (p1 == 'rock' and p2 == 'scissors') or \
       (p1 == 'scissors' and p2 == 'paper') or \
       (p1 == 'paper' and p2 == 'rock'):
        return 'player1'
    return 'player2'
exercises.test_code:
assert 'rps_winner' in dir(), "Function 'rps_winner' not defined. Did you write: def rps_winner(...)?"
_r0 = rps_winner('rock', 'rock')
assert _r0 == 'tie', f'Test 1: rps_winner('rock', 'rock') returned {_r0!r}, expected 'tie''
_r1 = rps_winner('rock', 'scissors')
assert _r1 == 'player1', f'Test 2: rps_winner('rock', 'scissors') returned {_r1!r}, expected 'player1''
_r2 = rps_winner('paper', 'rock')
assert _r2 == 'player1', f'Test 3: rps_winner('paper', 'rock') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'player1''
_r3 = rps_winner('rock', 'paper')
assert _r3 == 'player2', f'Test 4: rps_winner('rock', 'paper') returned {_r3!r}, expected 'player2''

exercises.title: Match statement (Python 3.10+)
exercises.description: Write a function using match/case that maps HTTP status codes to descriptions: 200->'OK', 301->'Moved', 400->'Bad Request', 404->'Not Found', 500->'Server Error', default->'Unknown'.
hints: Use match status_code: ... case ...
Cases can be single values or ranges (in this case, use if conditions inside case)
Use _ as default case
match/case is structural pattern matching in Python 3.10+
exercises.solution:
def http_status(code):
    match code:
        case 200:
            return 'OK'
        case 301:
            return 'Moved'
        case 400:
            return 'Bad Request'
        case 404:
            return 'Not Found'
        case 500:
            return 'Server Error'
        case _:
            return 'Unknown'
exercises.test_code:
assert 'http_status' in dir(), "Function 'http_status' not defined. Did you write: def http_status(...)?"

_r1 = http_status(404)
assert _r1 == 'Not Found', f'Test 2: http_status(404) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'Not Found''
_r2 = http_status(500)
assert _r2 == 'Server Error', f'Test 3: http_status(500) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'Server Error''
_r3 = http_status(999)
assert _r3 == 'Unknown', f'Test 4: http_status(999) returned {_r3!r}, expected 'Unknown''

exercises.title: Complex business logic: subscription tier upgrade

hints: Must not be upgrading from the same tier

Must either have verified payment OR sufficient credit balance
All three conditions must be true
exercises.solution:
def can_upgrade_tier(tier1, tier2, account_age, payment_verified, credit_balance):
    return (tier1 != tier2 and 
            account_age > 30 and 
            (payment_verified or credit_balance > 100))

_r3 = can_upgrade_tier('basic', 'basic', 60, True, 0)

hints: Use a nonlocal variable to modify the counter in the outer scope

exercises.solution:

    count = 0
    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count
    return increment

exercises.title: Compound Interest Calculator
exercises.description: Write a function `compound_interest(principal, rate, time, n=1)` that calculates final amount using A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). Default n=1 (annual).
hints: Use ** for exponentiation

exercises.solution:
def compound_interest(principal, rate, time, n=1):
    return principal * (1 + rate / n) ** (n * time)

exercises.title: Flexible Argument Unpacking
exercises.description: Write a function `flexible_sum(*args, **kwargs)` that sums all positional arguments and all values in kwargs. Example: flexible_sum(1, 2, a=3, b=4) returns 10.
hints: Sum the args tuple
Sum the values of kwargs dict
Add them together
exercises.solution:
def flexible_sum(*args, **kwargs):
    return sum(args) + sum(kwargs.values())
exercises.test_code:
assert 'flexible_sum' in dir(), "'flexible_sum' is not defined"
assert callable(flexible_sum), "'flexible_sum' should be a function"

exercises.title: Create a list

hints: Use square brackets [] to create a list.
Separate elements with commas.

exercises.title: Access list elements by index
exercises.description: Given the list items = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'], get the first element.
hints: List indices start at 0 in Python.

exercises.test_code:

exercises.title: Negative indexing
exercises.description: Given the list colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue', 'yellow'], get the last element using negative indexing.
hints: Negative indices count from the end of the list.

exercises.test_code:

exercises.title: Get list length
exercises.description: Write a function that returns the number of elements in a list.
hints: Use the len() function to get the length.

    return len(items)

exercises.title: Check if element in list

exercises.title: List slicing basics

exercises.title: Slice with step

exercises.title: Reverse a list with slicing

exercises.title: Basic list comprehension
exercises.description: Write a list comprehension that squares all numbers in a list.
hints: Use the syntax [expression for item in iterable].
The expression here is x**2.
exercises.solution:
def square_all(numbers):
    return [x**2 for x in numbers]

_r1 = square_all([0, 1, 2, 3])

_r2 = square_all([5])
assert _r2 == [25], f'Test 3: square_all([5]) returned {_r2!r}, expected [25]'

exercises.title: List comprehension with condition
exercises.description: Write a list comprehension that filters a list to keep only even numbers.
hints: Use [expression for item in iterable if condition].
The condition is x % 2 == 0.
exercises.solution:
def filter_even(numbers):
    return [x for x in numbers if x % 2 == 0]

_r1 = filter_even([2, 4, 6, 8])
assert _r1 == [2, 4, 6, 8], f'Test 2: filter_even([2, 4, 6, 8]) returned {_r1!r}, expected [2, 4, 6, 8]'
_r2 = filter_even([1, 3, 5])
assert _r2 == [], f'Test 3: filter_even([1, 3, 5]) returned {_r2!r}, expected []'

exercises.title: Filter function
exercises.description: Write a function that filters a list to keep only elements that satisfy a condition using filter().
hints: Use filter(func, list) where func returns True/False.
Convert to list for the result.
exercises.solution:
def filter_positive(numbers):
    return list(filter(lambda x: x > 0, numbers))

exercises.title: Flatten a nested list

hints: Use list comprehension with nested iteration.

exercises.solution:
def flatten(matrix):
    return [item for row in matrix for item in row]

exercises.title: Find common elements

exercises.title: Set add and discard

exercises.solution:
def modify_set(s, add_elem, discard_elem):
    s.add(add_elem)
    s.discard(discard_elem)
    return s

exercises.title: Leaderboard ranking
exercises.description: Write a function that takes a list of (player, score) tuples and returns a sorted leaderboard as a list of player names, highest score first. Players with the same score maintain original order.
hints: Use sorted() with a key function that sorts by score in reverse.

exercises.solution:

hints: Convert to sets and use set difference.

exercises.solution:

exercises.title: Matrix transpose
exercises.description: Write a function that transposes a 2D list (matrix). Rows become columns and columns become rows.
hints: Use zip() with unpacking: list(zip(*matrix)).

exercises.solution:
def transpose(matrix):
    return [list(row) for row in zip(*matrix)]

exercises.title: Filter Dictionary by Value
exercises.description: Write a function `filter_dict` that returns a new dict containing only items where value > threshold.
hints: Iterate through dict.items() to get key-value pairs
Use a dict comprehension or loop to build the result
exercises.solution:
def filter_dict(d, threshold):
    return {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > threshold}

exercises.title: Dictionary Comprehension
exercises.description: Write a function `square_values` that creates a new dict with the same keys but values squared.
hints: Use a dict comprehension: {k: expression for k, v in ...}
Iterate over dict.items()
exercises.solution:
def square_values(d):
    return {k: v ** 2 for k, v in d.items()}

exercises.title: Invert Dictionary

exercises.description: Write a function `validate_schema` that checks if a dict matches a schema (required keys).
hints: Schema is a list of required key names

exercises.solution:

exercises.title: Grade Book Statistics
exercises.description: Write a function `compute_stats` that takes grade_dict {student: [grades]} and returns {student: {mean, max, min}}.
hints: Compute mean, max, min for each student's grades
Return nested dict with stats
exercises.solution:
def compute_stats(grades_dict):
    return {student: {'mean': sum(grades)/len(grades), 'max': max(grades), 'min': min(grades)} for student, grades in grades_dict.items()}

exercises.title: Cross-Join Two Dicts
exercises.description: Write a function `cross_join` that creates all combinations of keys from two dicts with combined values.
hints: Use nested loops to iterate both dicts
Create key like (k1, k2) and value like {v1, v2}
exercises.solution:
def cross_join(d1, d2):
    return {(k1, k2): (v1, v2) for k1, v1 in d1.items() for k2, v2 in d2.items()}

class TaskStatus(str, Enum):

    IN_PROGRESS = 'in_progress'
    COMPLETED = 'completed'

class Task(BaseModel):
    title: str
    status: TaskStatus

exercises.description: Write a simple request logging function log_request(method: str, path: str) that formats a log entry.
hints: Return a formatted string with timestamp, method, path
Use datetime.now() for timestamp
exercises.solution:
from datetime import datetime

def log_request(method: str, path: str) -> str:
    timestamp = datetime.now().isoformat()
    return f'{timestamp} {method} {path}'

hints: Both filters must pass for item to be included

exercises.solution:
def filter_by_multiple(items: list, category: str, min_price: float) -> list:
    return [item for item in items if item.get('category') == category and item.get('price', 0) >= min_price]

exercises.title: What is an API? REST Basics
exercises.description: Understand what an API is. API stands for Application Programming Interface. REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to interact with resources. Given a description of what an action should do, identify the correct HTTP method.
hints: GET retrieves data (safe, idempotent)
POST creates new data (not idempotent)
PUT updates existing data (idempotent)
DELETE removes data (idempotent)
exercises.test_code:

_r1 = identify_http_method('create a new post')
assert _r1 == 'POST', f'Test 2: identify_http_method('create a new post') returned {_r1!r}, expected 'POST''
_r2 = identify_http_method('update user settings')
assert _r2 == 'PUT', f'Test 3: identify_http_method('update user settings') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'PUT''
_r3 = identify_http_method('remove a comment')
assert _r3 == 'DELETE', f'Test 4: identify_http_method('remove a comment') returned {_r3!r}, expected 'DELETE''
_r4 = identify_http_method('retrieve a list of items')
assert _r4 == 'GET', f'Test 5: identify_http_method('retrieve a list of items') returned {_r4!r}, expected 'GET''

exercises.title: Parse List from API Response

hints: Access the 'data' key from the response dict
Use len() to count items in the list

exercises.title: Build Basic Auth Header
exercises.description: Many APIs use Basic Authentication: combine username and password into a base64-encoded header. Build an Authorization header for Basic auth.
hints: Basic auth format: 'username:password'
Encode to base64: b64encode(b'username:password')
Header format: 'Basic ' + base64_string
exercises.solution:
from base64 import b64encode

def build_basic_auth(username, password):
    credentials = f"{username}:{password}"
    encoded = b64encode(credentials.encode()).decode()
    return f"Basic {encoded}"
exercises.test_code:
assert 'build_basic_auth' in dir(), "Function 'build_basic_auth' not defined. Did you write: def build_basic_auth(...)?"
_r0 = build_basic_auth('user', 'pass')
assert _r0 == 'Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz', f'Test 1: build_basic_auth('user', 'pass') returned {_r0!r}, expected 'Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz''
_r1 = build_basic_auth('admin', 'secret')
assert _r1 == 'Basic YWRtaW46c2VjcmV0', f'Test 2: build_basic_auth('admin', 'secret') returned {_r1!r}, expected 'Basic YWRtaW46c2VjcmV0''
_r2 = build_basic_auth('test', '123')
assert _r2 == 'Basic dGVzdDoxMjM=', f'Test 3: build_basic_auth('test', '123') returned {_r2!r}, expected 'Basic dGVzdDoxMjM=''

exercises.title: URL Encode Query Parameters

exercises.title: Build Complete API Request URL
exercises.description: Combine base URL, endpoint, and query parameters to build a complete API request URL.
hints: Pattern: base_url + '/' + endpoint + '?' + encoded_params
Use urlencode() for params
exercises.solution:
from urllib.parse import urlencode

def build_api_url(base_url, endpoint, params):
    encoded = urlencode(params)
    return f"{base_url}/{endpoint}?{encoded}"
exercises.test_code:

assert _r0 == 'https://api.github.com/users?per_page=10', f'Test 1: build_api_url('https://api.github.com', 'users', {'per_page': '10'}) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'https://api.github.com/users?per_page=10''
_r1 = build_api_url('https://api.example.com', 'search', {'q': 'python'})
assert _r1 == 'https://api.example.com/search?q=python', f'Test 2: build_api_url('https://api.example.com', 'search', {'q': 'python'}) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'https://api.example.com/search?q=python''
_r2 = build_api_url('https://api.test.org', 'data', {'id': '123', 'format': 'json'})
assert _r2 == 'https://api.test.org/data?id=123&format=json', f'Test 3: build_api_url('https://api.test.org', 'data', {'id': '123', 'format': 'json'}) returned {_r2!r}, expected 'https://api.test.org/data?id=123&format=json''

exercises.title: Filter Data from API Response
exercises.description: Extract and filter specific items from an API response. Get all users with age > 25.
hints: Use list comprehension: [item for item in list if condition]
Filter based on a field value
exercises.solution:
def filter_users_by_age(response, min_age):
    return [user for user in response['users'] if user['age'] > min_age]

_r1 = filter_users_by_age({'users': [{'name': 'Charlie', 'age': 26}, {'name': 'Diana', 'age': 28}, {'name': 'Eve', 'age': 22}]}, 25)
assert _r1 == [{'name': 'Charlie', 'age': 26}, {'name': 'Diana', 'age': 28}], f'Test 2: filter_users_by_age({'users': [{'name': 'Charlie', 'age': 26}, {'name': 'Diana', 'age': 28}, {'name': 'Eve', 'age': 22}]}, 25) returned {_r1!r}, expected [{'name': 'Charlie', 'age': 26}, {'name': 'Diana', 'age': 28}]'

exercises.title: Validate Required Fields in Response
exercises.description: Ensure API response has required fields. Check if 'status' and 'data' keys exist; return True if valid, False otherwise.
hints: Check if keys exist in dict using 'in' operator

exercises.solution:
def validate_response(response, required_fields):
    return all(field in response for field in required_fields)

exercises.title: Exponential Backoff Delay Calculation
exercises.description: When rate limited, retry with increasing delays. Calculate exponential backoff: delay = base * (multiplier ^ attempt).
hints: Formula: base * (multiplier ** attempt)
Common: base=1, multiplier=2 (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...)
exercises.solution:
def calculate_backoff(attempt, base=1, multiplier=2):
    return base * (multiplier ** attempt)
exercises.test_code:
assert 'calculate_backoff' in dir(), "Function 'calculate_backoff' not defined. Did you write: def calculate_backoff(...)?"
_r0 = calculate_backoff(0, 1, 2)
assert _r0 == 1, f'Test 1: calculate_backoff(0, 1, 2) returned {_r0!r}, expected 1'
_r1 = calculate_backoff(1, 1, 2)
assert _r1 == 2, f'Test 2: calculate_backoff(1, 1, 2) returned {_r1!r}, expected 2'
_r2 = calculate_backoff(3, 1, 2)
assert _r2 == 8, f'Test 3: calculate_backoff(3, 1, 2) returned {_r2!r}, expected 8'
_r3 = calculate_backoff(2, 2, 3)
assert _r3 == 18, f'Test 4: calculate_backoff(2, 2, 3) returned {_r3!r}, expected 18'

exercises.title: Build API Cache Key
exercises.description: Implement response caching by building unique cache keys from request parameters.
hints: Cache key should include base URL and all params
Use stable string representation (sorted dicts)
exercises.solution:
def build_cache_key(endpoint, params):
    sorted_params = sorted(params.items())
    param_str = "&".join(f"{k}={v}" for k, v in sorted_params)
    return f"{endpoint}?{param_str}"
exercises.test_code:
assert 'build_cache_key' in dir(), "Function 'build_cache_key' not defined. Did you write: def build_cache_key(...)?"
_r0 = build_cache_key('https://api.example.com/users', {'id': '1'})
assert _r0 == 'https://api.example.com/users?id=1', f'Test 1: build_cache_key('https://api.example.com/users', {'id': '1'}) returned {_r0!r}, expected 'https://api.example.com/users?id=1''
_r1 = build_cache_key('https://api.example.com/search', {'q': 'test', 'page': '1'})
assert _r1 == 'https://api.example.com/search?page=1&q=test', f'Test 2: build_cache_key('https://api.example.com/search', {'q': 'test', 'page': '1'}) returned {_r1!r}, expected 'https://api.example.com/search?page=1&q=test''

hints: Could be string or Unix timestamp
Validate presence before using

    return sum(1 for item in response['items'] if item['status'] == status)

    return sum(item['value'] for response in responses for item in response['items'])

exercises.title: Handle Webhook Retry Validation
exercises.description: Webhook request includes attempt number and max attempts. Validate that retry attempt is valid.
hints: Check X-Webhook-Attempt header
Check X-Webhook-Retry-Count header
Validate attempt <= max_attempts
exercises.solution:
def validate_webhook_attempt(headers):
    attempt = int(headers.get('X-Webhook-Attempt', 1))
    max_attempts = int(headers.get('X-Webhook-Retry-Count', 3))
    return attempt <= max_attempts

_r1 = validate_webhook_attempt({'X-Webhook-Attempt': '4', 'X-Webhook-Retry-Count': '3'})
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: validate_webhook_attempt({'X-Webhook-Attempt': '4', 'X-Webhook-Retry-Count': '3'}) returned {_r1!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Implement Sliding Window Rate Limiter
exercises.description: Implement sliding window rate limiter: track requests in time window, reject if exceeds limit.
hints: Keep list of request timestamps
Remove timestamps outside window
Check count against limit
exercises.solution:
def sliding_window_ratelimit(requests, limit, window_seconds):
    # requests is list of timestamps
    # Remove old timestamps - keep only those within the window
    if not requests:
        return True
    cutoff = requests[-1] - window_seconds
    recent = [r for r in requests if r > cutoff]
    # Return True if count exceeds limit
    return len(recent) > limit
exercises.test_code:
assert 'sliding_window_ratelimit' in dir(), "Function 'sliding_window_ratelimit' not defined. Did you write: def sliding_window_ratelimit(...)?"
_r0 = sliding_window_ratelimit([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3, 10)
assert _r0 == True, f'Test 1: sliding_window_ratelimit([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3, 10) returned {_r0!r}, expected True'
_r1 = sliding_window_ratelimit([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], 5, 3)
assert _r1 == False, f'Test 2: sliding_window_ratelimit([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], 5, 3) returned {_r1!r}, expected False'

exercises.title: Build GraphQL Query
exercises.description: GraphQL APIs use queries instead of URLs. Build a GraphQL query string.
hints: GraphQL syntax: query { field { subfield } }
Variables can be passed separately
exercises.solution:
def build_graphql_query(fields, filters):
    filter_str = ", ".join(f"{k}: {v}" for k, v in filters.items())
    field_str = " ".join(fields)
    return f"query {{ users({filter_str}) {{ {field_str} }} }}"

exercises.title: Process Streaming API Response
exercises.description: Streaming APIs send data in chunks. Process each chunk and accumulate results.
hints: Each chunk is a JSON line or partial response
Accumulate into final result
Handle incomplete chunks
exercises.solution:
import json

def process_stream(*chunks):
    result = []
    for chunk in chunks:
        data = json.loads(chunk)
        result.append(data)
    return result
